nativeRange.habitat: Deepwater coral reefs, seamounts, and offshore banks; recorded depth range generally 64–250 m (with reports to ~250 m / ~820 ft in Tahiti)

diet.notes: Feeds primarily on small fishes and crustaceans in the wild. In captivity, generally accepts thawed marine meats such as silversides, krill (preferably soaked in a vitamin supplement such as Selcon), squid, prawn, clam, mussel, and chopped marine fish. Large sinking pellets have been used as supplements. Feeder fish (especially freshwater feeders such as goldfish) are best avoided as a primary food. Infrequent, larger meals tend to be advised over frequent small feedings.

goodWith: robust predatory fish of comparable size
avoidWith: anything that fits in its mouth

summary: An extremely rare deepwater grouper from the western Pacific, prized for its vivid purple-and-gold coloration. Generally considered an aggressive ambush predator that has been reported to consume any fish or crustacean small enough to fit in its mouth.
careNotes:
The Neptune Grouper (Cephalopholis igarashiensis) is one of the rarest and most expensive groupers in the aquarium trade, sourced from deepwater reefs (typically 64–250 m) across the western Pacific from southern Japan through the Philippines, Taiwan, Guam, Fiji, Samoa, and French Polynesia. Specimens are commonly considered advanced-care due to the species' deepwater origin, large adult size, and demanding feeding/water-quality requirements rather than any particular husbandry fragility once acclimated.

Tank-size recommendations vary substantially across sources. LiveAquaria and Quality Marine list a 180-gallon (~681 L) minimum with the caveat that 'bigger is better'; Reeflex and several specialist write-ups recommend at least 3000 L. The figure used here (~1000 L / ~265 gal) reflects a cautious-larger compromise consistent with the species' adult size (commonly 25 cm but up to ~43 cm), high waste output, and need for swimming/territorial space; a reviewer should consider whether 1500–2000 L is more appropriate for long-term housing. Tank-size spread exceeds 2x across sources — flagged for review.

This species originates from dim, deepwater habitats and has been reported to be stressed by the high-intensity lighting common in reef aquariums. Subdued lighting and ample shaded rockwork (caves, overhangs) tend to be advised. It is generally described as an ambush predator that may claim a single cave as a territory and defend it, with otherwise relatively reserved behaviour in the open. Reef compatibility is listed as 'with caution' because the species poses minimal threat to corals but will readily consume ornamental shrimp, small fish, and other inverts.

The Cephalopholis genus is broadly protogynous (female-first sequential hermaphrodites), and the same is likely for this species, though it has not been directly confirmed in the sources consulted. Captive breeding has not been documented. Juveniles display markedly different coloration from adults, including a prominent black eyespot on the soft-rayed portion of the dorsal fin.

Cross-source data: temperature ranged 18.3–26.5°C across FishBase wild data and aquarium-hobby sources (22.2–25.6°C from LiveAquaria); the averaged 22–26°C used here favours the captive-recommended band. Adult-size figures converged on 25 cm common to 43 cm max (Wikipedia, FishBase, Reeflex), with LiveAquaria's 18 in (~46 cm) consistent with the upper bound.
breedingNotes: Captive breeding of Cephalopholis igarashiensis has not been documented in the hobby. The species is wild-collected from deepwater habitats, and most Cephalopholis groupers are believed to be protogynous hermaphrodites, but this has not been directly confirmed for igarashiensis in the sources consulted.

primary.name: LiveAquaria
primary.notes: Primary source for husbandry recommendations: tank minimum (180 gal), water parameters (72–78°F, pH 8.1–8.4, SG 1.020–1.025, dKH 8–12), temperament (aggressive), reef-safe (with caution), and diet.

additional.name: FishBase
additional.notes: Cross-checked taxonomy (Epinephelidae / Perciformes), maximum size (43 cm TL, common 25 cm), depth range (80–250 m), wild temperature range (18.3–26.5°C, mean 21.3°C), and native range.

additional.name: Wikipedia
additional.notes: Cross-checked taxonomy, maximum standard length (43 cm), depth range (64–250 m), western-Pacific distribution, and the juvenile-vs-adult color phase (juveniles show a black eyespot on the dorsal soft rays).

additional.name: Quality Marine
additional.notes: Cross-checked tank size (180 gal min, 'bigger is better'), confirmed deepwater origin and reported sensitivity to bright reef-aquarium lighting, and described territorial cave-claiming behaviour.

additional.name: Reeflex
additional.notes: Cross-checked adult size (25–43 cm), depth (80–250 m), diet (fishes, crustaceans, mysis), aggressive temperament, and the much-larger tank recommendation of at least 3000 L — used to bias the tank-size pick upward.

fish.breedingNotes: Captive breeding has not been documented in the hobby. Most Cephalopholis are protogynous hermaphrodites, but this has not been directly confirmed for C. igarashiensis.
